name: graph
description: Canvas + React graph/flow diagram library with Preact signals for reactive block state, ELK auto-layout, custom HTML block rendering, layers, and portals.
when_to_use: Canvas + React graph/flow diagram library with Preact signals for reactive block state, ELK auto-layout, custom HTML block rendering, layers, and portals
avoid: Not for charts or data visualizations — use chartkit/charts/yagr. Not for timeline visualizations — use @gravity-ui/timeline.

name: applyAlpha
description: Applies an alpha (opacity) value to a CSS color string, returning a new rgba color string. Used for rendering semi-transparent graph elements like hovered or dimmed blocks.
when_to_use: Creating transparent variants of graph theme colors in canvas render methods
signature: applyAlpha(color: string, alpha: number): string

parameters.name: color
parameters.description: CSS color string (hex, rgb, rgba, or named).

name: clearColorCache
description: Clears the internal color parsing cache used by applyAlpha. Call this when theme colors change to ensure subsequent applyAlpha calls use updated values.
when_to_use: After programmatically changing graph colors via graph.setColors(), to avoid stale cached color values
signature: clearColorCache(): void

name: Component
description: Base class for all canvas-rendered components in the graph library. Extends CoreComponent with lifecycle hooks (willMount, willRender, didRender, willUpdateChildren, didUpdateChildren), state/props management via setState/setProps, and context propagation. All graph canvas elements (blocks, connections, layers) extend this class.
when_to_use: Extending to create custom canvas-rendered graph components
Understanding the lifecycle and rendering model of graph internals
avoid: Using Component directly — extend Layer or GraphComponent for custom extensions
Calling render() directly — use performRender() to schedule updates
Setting this.state or this.props directly — use setState() and setProps()
signature: class Component<Props, State, Context> extends CoreComponent

name: debounce
description: Scheduler-aware debounce utility from the graph library. Delays function execution until a minimum number of animation frames and an optional timeout have passed since the last call. Returns the debounced function with a cancel() method.
when_to_use: Debouncing handlers in Layer classes tied to the graph animation frame cycle
Delaying expensive canvas operations triggered by frequent events
avoid: For time-only debouncing outside the graph context — use lodash debounce instead
signature: debounce<T extends (...args: unknown[]) => void>(fn: T, options: TDebounceOptions): T & { cancel(): void }

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Options: priority (ESchedulerPriority), frameInterval (number of frames), frameTimeout (ms).

name: Graph
description: Core graph instance class. Manages the scheduler, camera, layers, stores (blocks, connections, settings), event system, and API. Created directly or via useGraph hook. Provides methods to attach to DOM, start/stop rendering, add layers, set colors/constants, and zoom.
when_to_use: When you need direct access to the graph instance outside of React (e.g., imperative control)
Inside custom Layer classes via this.props.graph to access the API, rootStore, or camera
avoid: Creating Graph directly in React components — use useGraph hook instead for lifecycle management
signature: new Graph(config: TGraphConfig, rootEl?: HTMLDivElement, graphColors?: TGraphColors, graphConstants?: TGraphConstants): Graph

parameters.name: config
parameters.description: Graph configuration including blocks, connections, settings, and layers.

parameters.name: rootEl
parameters.description: DOM element to attach the graph to immediately.

examples:
// Inside a Layer, access graph API:
this.props.graph.rootStore.blocksList.getBlocks();

name: GraphBlock
description: Base block component for rendering an HTML block via React inside the graph canvas. Creates a correctly positioned container for a graph block, updates geometry and z-index when state changes, and hides the canvas block once the React version renders.
when_to_use: When rendering custom HTML/React content inside a graph block
When you need rich interactive UI inside a node (buttons, inputs, rich text)
As the wrapper component inside the renderBlock callback of GraphCanvas
avoid: Do not use outside of the renderBlock callback — block context requires an active graph instance
Do not use for purely canvas-rendered blocks at low zoom — GraphBlock only activates at detailed zoom level

props.name: block
props.description: Block instance from the graph store.

props.name: children
props.description: Content to render inside the block wrapper.

props.name: graph
props.description: Graph instance providing context for block state.

props.name: autoHideCanvas
props.description: Automatically hides the canvas block when the React block renders. Defaults to true.

props.name: canvasVisible
props.description: Manually controls canvas block visibility when autoHideCanvas is false.

props.name: className
props.description: CSS class applied to the inner wrapper div.

props.name: containerClassName
props.description: CSS class applied to the outer container div that handles position and size.

examples: Block name: {block.name}

name: GraphBlockAnchor
description: Renders an anchor (connection point) for a graph block as a React component. Synchronizes position with the canvas anchor state and applies selection and raised states automatically.
when_to_use: When rendering custom HTML anchors/ports on a GraphBlock
When you need interactive or styled connection points on a node
Inside a renderBlock callback alongside GraphBlock
avoid: Do not use outside of a GraphBlock — anchor position is relative to the parent block geometry
Do not manually set anchor position via CSS; the component uses CSS variables to synchronize with canvas coordinates

props.name: anchor
props.description: Anchor data object from the block's anchors array.

props.name: graph
props.description: Graph instance used to look up anchor state.

props.name: position
props.description: Positioning mode for the anchor element.

props.name: children
props.description: Static content or a render-prop function that receives the current anchor state.

props.name: className
props.description: Additional CSS class for the anchor container.

name: GraphCanvas
description: Main React container component for the graph. Attaches a Graph instance to the DOM, sets up the ReactLayer for HTML block rendering, wires CSS color variables, and forwards graph events via callback props.
when_to_use: As the top-level component for rendering any graph
When you have a Graph instance created via useGraph and want to mount it into the React tree
When you need to pass graph event callbacks (click, drag, selection) declaratively
avoid: Do not nest multiple GraphCanvas instances — each manages its own Graph DOM attachment
Do not use without a Graph instance; always pair with useGraph

props.name: graph
props.description: Graph instance to mount; typically obtained from useGraph.

props.name: blockListClassName
props.description: CSS class forwarded to the block list layer.

props.name: children
props.description: Additional React content rendered inside the graph wrapper (e.g., GraphPortal, GraphLayer).

props.name: className
props.description: CSS class for the outermost wrapper div.

props.name: click
props.description: Called when the graph canvas is clicked.

props.name: dblclick
props.description: Called on double-click.

props.name: onBlockChange
props.description: Called when a block's state changes.

props.name: onBlockDrag
props.description: Called while a block is being dragged.

props.name: onBlockDragEnd
props.description: Called when a block drag ends.

props.name: onBlockDragStart
props.description: Called when a block drag starts.

props.name: onBlockSelectionChange
props.description: Called when block selection changes.

props.name: onCameraChange
props.description: Called on camera pan or zoom.

props.name: onConnectionSelectionChange
props.description: Called when connection selection changes.

props.name: onStateChanged
props.description: Called when the graph's lifecycle state changes.

props.name: reactLayerRef
props.description: Ref to access the internal ReactLayer instance.

props.name: renderBlock
props.description: Render function called for each visible block; return a GraphBlock element.

name: GraphLayer
description: Declarative React component that adds an existing Layer class to the graph. Exposes the layer instance via a ref. Renders no visible DOM — it is a side-effect component used inside GraphCanvas children.
when_to_use: When you want to add a custom Layer class to the graph declaratively inside JSX
When you need a ref to the layer instance for imperative access
As an alternative to the useLayer hook when working in a component that already has GraphCanvas as ancestor
avoid: Do not use outside of GraphCanvas — it reads graph from GraphContext and will throw without a provider
Prefer useLayer when you need the layer instance during render (e.g., in the same component)

props.name: layer
props.description: Layer class constructor to instantiate and add to the graph.

props.name: props
props.description: Custom props forwarded to the layer constructor (exclude graph, camera, root, emitter).

props.name: ref
props.description: Ref to access the created layer instance.

name: GraphPortal
description: Declarative component for creating an HTML layer inside the graph using a React Portal. Renders children into a managed Layer HTML element without requiring a custom Layer class, supporting camera-following overlays.
when_to_use: When you need to render arbitrary React UI inside the graph layer stack
When you want a floating overlay that optionally follows the camera
As an alternative to writing a full custom Layer class for simple HTML overlays
avoid: Do not use outside of GraphCanvas — it reads graph from GraphContext
Do not use for block-level rendering — use GraphBlock inside renderBlock instead

props.name: children
props.description: Content to render in the portal, or a render-prop function receiving layer and graph.

props.name: className
props.description: CSS class added to the layer HTML element.

props.name: transformByCameraPosition
props.description: When true, the portal layer follows camera pan and zoom. Defaults to false.

props.name: zIndex
props.description: Z-index for the portal layer. Defaults to 100.

examples: Custom Overlay

name: useBlockAnchorState
description: Returns the reactive AnchorState for a given anchor on a block. Subscribes to signal updates so the component re-renders when the anchor state changes.
when_to_use: When you need to read anchor selection, position, or other state reactively in a React component
Inside a custom GraphBlockAnchor implementation
avoid: Do not use for block-level state — use useBlockState instead
signature: useBlockAnchorState(graph: Graph, anchor: TAnchor): AnchorState | undefined

parameters.name: anchor
parameters.description: Anchor object whose state to subscribe to.

name: useBlockState
description: Returns the reactive BlockState for a given block ID or block object. Subscribes to signal updates and triggers re-renders when the block state changes.
when_to_use: When you need to access block geometry, selection, or other reactive state in a React component
Inside a custom block renderer alongside GraphBlock
avoid: Do not use outside a Graph context — the graph's rootStore must be initialized
signature: useBlockState<T extends TBlock>(graph: Graph, block: T | T['id']): BlockState | undefined

name: useComputedSignal
description: Subscribes to a derived (computed) signal created from a compute function and dependency list. Re-renders the component whenever the computed signal value changes.
when_to_use: When you need to derive a value from one or more signals and keep it reactive
As a more flexible alternative to useSignal when the signal itself is not stable
avoid: Do not use for simple static values — use useMemo instead
Avoid heavy computations in the compute function; it runs on every signal change
signature: useComputedSignal<T>(compute: () => T, deps: DependencyList): T

parameters.name: compute
parameters.description: Function that reads signals and returns a derived value.

parameters.name: deps
parameters.description: React dependency array that recreates the computed signal when changed.

name: useElk
description: Runs an ELK (Eclipse Layout Kernel) graph layout computation and returns the converted result as blocks and connections compatible with the graph library. Handles async loading state and cancellation on re-runs.
when_to_use: When you want automatic graph layout using ELK (hierarchical, force-directed, etc.)
When converting an ELK node graph into block/connection entities for the graph library
avoid: Do not use without an initialized ELK instance — the caller must supply the elk argument
Not suitable for real-time interactive layout — ELK is async and best for initial placement
signature: useElk(config: ElkNode, elk: ELK, args?: ElkLayoutArguments & { onError?: (e: Error) => void }): { result: ConverterResult | null; isLoading: boolean }

parameters.name: config
parameters.description: ELK node configuration describing the graph topology.

parameters.name: args
parameters.description: Optional layout arguments and error callback.

name: useGraph
description: Creates and manages a Graph instance for use in React. Returns the graph and a set of stable API methods for setting entities, updating settings, managing layers, and controlling zoom. Automatically cleans up the graph on unmount.
when_to_use: To create and control a Graph instance inside a React component
As the primary entry point before rendering GraphCanvas
When you need access to graph API methods like setEntities, zoomTo, start
avoid: Do not call multiple times for the same graph — the hook memoizes by config.graph reference
Do not pass new object literals as config on each render without useMemo — settings will update on every render
signature: useGraph(config: HookGraphParams): { graph: Graph; api: PublicGraphApi; setSettings, start, stop, setViewConfiguration, addLayer, setEntities, updateEntities, zoomTo }

parameters.name: config
parameters.description: Configuration including settings, layers, viewConfiguration, and an optional existing graph instance.

return_type: { graph: Graph; api: PublicGraphApi; setSettings: (s) => void; start: () => void; stop: () => void; setViewConfiguration: (v) => void; addLayer: (ctor, props) => Layer; setEntities: (entities) => void; updateEntities: (entities) => void; zoomTo: (target, config?) => void }

name: useGraphEvent
description: Subscribes to a single graph event and calls the provided callback when it fires. Supports optional debouncing via scheduler frame intervals. Automatically unsubscribes on unmount.
when_to_use: When you need to react to a specific graph event in a React component
For camera changes, block interactions, or state transitions
avoid: Do not use for many events at once — use useGraphEvents for bulk subscriptions
Do not pass unstable callbacks without useFn or useCallback — it will resubscribe on each render
signature: useGraphEvent<Event extends keyof GraphEventsDefinitions>(graph: Graph | null, event: Event, cb: (data, event) => void, debounceParams?: object): void

name: useGraphEvents
description: Subscribes to multiple graph events at once using a callback map. Each key corresponds to a TGraphEventCallbacks key and maps to the underlying graph event. Automatically cleans up on unmount.
when_to_use: When passing multiple event callbacks to a graph in one call
Inside GraphCanvas-like wrappers that accept a spread of callback props
avoid: Do not reconstruct the events object on every render without memoization — causes constant resubscription
signature: useGraphEvents(graph: Graph | null, events: Partial<TGraphEventCallbacks>): void

name: useLayer
description: Adds a Layer class to the graph and returns the layer instance. Automatically initializes when the graph is ready and detaches the layer on unmount. Uses deep equality to update layer props without unnecessary re-initialization.
when_to_use: When you need to add a custom Layer to the graph from a React component
When you want automatic layer cleanup tied to component lifecycle
avoid: Do not pass graph, camera, root, or emitter in props — these are auto-provided
Do not use when the layer is needed only declaratively inside GraphCanvas children — use GraphLayer component instead
signature: useLayer<T extends Constructor<Layer>>(graph: Graph | null, layerCtor: T, props: LayerPublicProps<T>): InstanceType<T> | null

parameters.name: graph
parameters.description: Graph instance to add the layer to.

parameters.name: props
parameters.description: Custom props for the layer (excluding internal props).

name: useSceneChange
description: Calls the provided function whenever the visible scene changes — either via camera movement or block position updates. Uses scheduler debouncing for high-priority updates and subscribes to both camera-change events and hitTest updates.
when_to_use: When you need to respond to any visible change in the graph viewport (pan, zoom, block move)
For updating overlays or external UI that mirrors the graph viewport
avoid: Do not use for block-specific updates — use useBlockState or useSignal instead
signature: useSceneChange(graph: Graph, fn: () => void): void

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: Callback invoked on any scene change.

name: useSchedulerDebounce
description: Returns a debounced version of a function that delays execution until both a minimum number of animation frames and an optional timeout have elapsed. Automatically cancels on unmount.
when_to_use: When you need to debounce a handler tied to the graph scheduler's frame cycle
For expensive updates triggered by frequent events like mouse move or camera change
avoid: Do not use for non-scheduler logic — prefer standard lodash debounce for time-only debouncing
signature: useSchedulerDebounce<T extends (...args) => void>(fn: T, options: TDebounceOptions): T & { cancel(): void }

name: useScheduledTask
description: Schedules a task for one-time execution after the specified number of animation frames. Automatically cancels the scheduled task on unmount.
when_to_use: When you need to defer a one-time action to a future animation frame
For post-render tasks that must happen after the scheduler cycle
avoid: Do not use for repeating tasks — use useSchedulerDebounce or useSchedulerThrottle
signature: useScheduledTask<T extends (...args: unknown[]) => void>(fn: T, options: Omit<TScheduleOptions, 'once'>): void

name: useSchedulerThrottle
description: Returns a throttled version of a function that limits execution to at most once per specified frame interval and timeout. Unlike debounce, executes immediately on the first call. Automatically cancels on unmount.
when_to_use: When you want to rate-limit a handler to fire at most once per N frames
For real-time interactions where the first response should be immediate but subsequent calls throttled
avoid: Do not use when you want to wait for the last call — use useSchedulerDebounce instead
signature: useSchedulerThrottle<T extends (...args) => void>(fn: T, options: TDebounceOptions): T & { cancel(): void }

name: useSignal
description: Subscribes a React component to a Preact signal and returns its current value. Re-renders the component whenever the signal value changes, using useSyncExternalStore for safe concurrent-mode access.
when_to_use: When you need to read a graph signal value and re-render on change
For block geometry, selection state, or any other signal exposed by the graph store
avoid: Do not use with unstable signal references — ensure the signal object is stable across renders
signature: useSignal<T>(signal: Signal<T>): T

name: useSignalEffect
description: Runs an effect function reactively whenever any signals read inside it change. Similar to Preact's effect() but integrated with React's useEffect and dependency list for controlling when the effect is recreated.
when_to_use: When you need to apply side effects (DOM mutations, style updates) in response to signal changes
Inside block components that update CSS variables based on geometry signals
avoid: Do not use for derived values that need to be returned — use useComputedSignal instead
Avoid reading non-signal reactive state inside the effect function
signature: useSignalEffect(effectFn: () => void, deps: DependencyList): void

parameters.name: effectFn
parameters.description: Effect function that reads signals; re-runs when signals change.

parameters.name: deps
parameters.description: React dependency array that recreates the effect subscription.

name: Layer
description: Base class for all graph rendering layers. Extends Component with Canvas and HTML element management, camera transform synchronization, event subscription helpers (onGraphEvent, onCanvasEvent, onHtmlEvent, onRootEvent, onSignal), and automatic AbortController cleanup on unmount. All custom graph extensions should extend Layer.
when_to_use: Creating custom rendering layers that add canvas drawing or HTML overlays to the graph
Adding new interaction behaviors or visual elements via the layer system
avoid: Setting up event subscriptions in the constructor — always use afterInit()
Passing graph, camera, root to addLayer() — these are auto-provided
Calling render() directly — use performRender()
signature: class Layer<Props extends LayerProps, Context extends LayerContext, State> extends Component

name: schedule
description: Scheduler-aware one-shot task scheduler from the graph library. Schedules a function to run once after a specified number of animation frames. Returns a cancel function.
when_to_use: Deferring a one-time action to a future animation frame within the graph scheduler
avoid: For repeating tasks — use debounce or throttle instead
For non-scheduler deferred logic — use requestAnimationFrame directly
signature: schedule(fn: () => void, options: TScheduleOptions): () => void

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: Options: priority (ESchedulerPriority), frameInterval (number of frames to wait).

name: throttle
description: Scheduler-aware throttle utility from the graph library. Limits function execution to at most once per specified frame interval, executing immediately on the first call. Returns the throttled function with a cancel() method.
when_to_use: Rate-limiting canvas handlers where the first call should respond immediately but subsequent calls are throttled
avoid: When you want to wait for the last call — use debounce instead
signature: throttle<T extends (...args: unknown[]) => void>(fn: T, options: TDebounceOptions): T & { cancel(): void }
